summary: Gruber draws attention to the gendered language used by tech publications when describing departing Apple executives. He contrasts how Peter Oppenheimer was described by his title 'Apple CFO' while Katie Cotton was labeled 'PR Queen,' and finds the same pattern with Angela Ahrendts being called 'Queen of Apple Retail.' He argues that 'queen' is not a neutral shorthand for authority — it emphasizes gender and carries connotations of entitlement and unearned status. He concludes that unintentional sexism is still sexism, and that job descriptions should not change based on the gender of the person holding the position.
key_points: Tech publications used the neutral title 'CFO' for Peter Oppenheimer but called Katie Cotton 'PR Queen' — a gendered and loaded term
The same pattern appeared with Angela Ahrendts being labeled 'Queen of Apple Retail' where a male predecessor was simply called 'boss'
Words like boss, chief, head, and leader convey authority neutrally, while 'queen' emphasizes gender and implies arrogance, entitlement, or unearned status
Unintentional sexism is still sexism — there's almost never a reason to use different words for a woman's job than you'd use for a man's
money_quotes: My point was to draw attention to the disparate job descriptions: 'Apple CFO' vs. 'PR Queen'.
Boss, chief, head, leader — honcho perhaps, if you want to be casual — any of these words can be used to convey authority.
Queen, though, emphasizes something else: gender.
It carries other connotations, none of them flattering: queens are arrogant, distant, prissy, entitled, superior; they become queens by birthright or marriage, not through merit.
There's almost never a good reason to use a different word to describe a woman's job than the words you'd choose if the position were held by a man.
You know you're in poor company when you've chosen the same word as Valleywag's Sam Biddle.
key_insight: When publications reach for gendered words like 'queen' instead of neutral terms like 'chief' or 'boss' to describe women executives, they reveal an unintentional but real sexism that undermines the authority those women earned on merit.
